text: We present the case of a diagnostic laparoscopy with a desiolysis for intestinal obstruction in a patient with status post open hysterectomy. Forty-two year old patient, a post open hysterectomy done one week back, presented with abdominal distension, nausea, vomiting, constipation and obstipation since post op day two. Patient was tender, guarded and distended. X-ray was suggestive of air fluid levels with no gas under diaphragm.
The decision was taken to do an urgent diagnostic laparoscopy in view of adhesive intestinal obstruction.

The surgeon enters the abdomen using a Veress needle at Palmer's point, followed by the placement of two working ports on the left flank. Multiple omental adhesions are visible attached to the anterior abdominal wall, which are carefully dissected and removed.

After completing the omental adhesiolysis, the surgeon visualizes a collapsed small bowel loop near the vaginal vault. Flimsy omental adhesions to the small bowel loops are carefully dissected and removed.
The surgeon identifies the left ovary and removes omental adhesions attached to it. The omentum is repositioned to one side, allowing examination of the distended small bowel.

The surgeon carefully traces the distended small bowel using graspers, initially moving proximally and then distally to identify the transition zone, ensuring gentle handling to prevent perforation of the bowel loops.
The surgeon traces the distended small bowel distally, identifying the transition zone at the level of the vaginal vault, where possible adhesions are causing obstruction.
The surgeon carefully releases small bowel adhesions near the vaginal vault, where a hemostatic agent is visible, potentially contributing to adhesion formation.

The surgeon identifies a dense fibrotic band of adhesions on the left lateral side of the vaginal vault, likely resulting from previous suture repair. Sharp dissection is attempted using cold scissors, but the thickness of the band makes the dissection challenging.

The surgeon attempts a different approach to release the dense fibrotic adhesions and the small bowel loop. Sutures are visible over the vaginal vault, along with a hemostatic agent applied to control bleeding during the index surgery.
The surgeon performs further adhesiolysis to release the small bowel from adhesions around the vaginal vault and the right ovary, carefully tracing the bowel distally up to the ileocecal junction.

The surgeon performs intervowel adhesiolysis using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to release the small bowel, tracing the collapsed loops distally to the ileocecal junction. The examination reveals a coiled loop of the small bowel, initially suspected to be a stricture.

The surgeon inspects the vaginal vault for loose sutures and identifies a small indirect left inguinal hernia. A suture repair is performed on the hernia, opting against mesh repair due to the presence of acute intestinal obstruction and elevated counts indicating a septic state.
The surgeon carefully takes thin bites of the peritoneum using a surgical instrument, ensuring precision to avoid injury to the underlying nerves.

Once the suture repair was done, the abdomen was desufflated after placing a drain. The patient was started on a clear liquid diet on post-op day one, passed flatus and stools on post-op day two, and was switched to a soft diet on post-op day three and discharged.
